briefs.title: Burnaby shelter at 3020 Gilmore cannot open until Good Neighbour and Operational Plans approved
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has decided that BC Housing and the shelter operator must submit two plans — a Good Neighbour Plan and an Operational Management Plan — and get City approval before the 3020 Gilmore Diversion shelter can open. Council also asked BC Housing to do a more thorough round of public consultation before moving forward.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near 3020 Gilmore, monitor City updates on plan approvals and public consultation opportunities.

briefs.title: Burnaby rezones 8265–8303 10th Avenue — land use rules at this address have changed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has given final approval to Bylaw #14780, which changes the zoning (the official rules about how land can be used or developed) for properties at 8265–8303 10th Avenue. Any business owner, landlord, or developer with an interest in these specific properties is now subject to the new land use rules.
briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to develop property at 8265–8303 10th Avenue, contact Burnaby Planning to confirm what the new zoning allows.

briefs.title: Burnaby plumbing permit fees updated — costs change for construction projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has passed an amendment to its Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, changing the fees charged for plumbing permits. If you are planning any construction, renovation, or plumbing work in Burnaby that requires a permit, the cost of that permit has changed.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated Burnaby plumbing permit fee schedule before budgeting your next construction or renovation project.

briefs.title: Burnaby Bear Spray Bylaw Amendment Passes All Three Readings
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has passed all three readings of an amendment to its Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, adding new rules around bear spray. This means bear spray is now subject to local bylaw enforcement in Burnaby, and violations can result in bylaw fines (tickets issued by the city, not criminal charges).
briefs.action_required: Check if your business sells or allows bear spray on premises and review Burnaby's updated bylaw rules.
briefs.industries: Retail, Grocery & Food Retail, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Burnaby now regulates bear spray sales under its Business Licence Bylaw
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of Bylaw #14798, which adds bear spray sales to the rules businesses must follow under the Burnaby Business Licence Bylaw 2017. Retailers who sell bear spray in Burnaby are now subject to new licensing conditions tied to that product.
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby to confirm what licence conditions now apply to your bear spray sales.

briefs.title: Burnaby North Road BIA Bylaw 2026 approved — what local businesses need to know
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of the Burnaby Business Improvement Area (North Road) Bylaw 2026. This formally renews or establishes the North Road BIA (a Business Improvement Area — a defined zone where local businesses collectively fund neighbourhood improvements and promotions) for 2026.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is within the North Road BIA zone, as you may owe a BIA levy in 2026.

briefs.title: Burnaby rezones 7305 & 7317 Buller Ave — first and second reading passed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has passed the first and second reading of Bylaw #14794, which proposes to rezone (change the permitted land use) for two properties at 7305 and 7317 Buller Avenue. The bylaw must still complete further readings and approval before the rezoning is final.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council agendas for the public hearing and third reading dates if this rezoning affects your business.

briefs.title: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw text amendment passes first and second reading
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has given first and second reading to Bylaw No. 14792, which makes a text amendment (a written change to the rules) to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965. This is an early step in the approval process — the bylaw is not yet in force, but it is moving forward.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council updates for third reading and final adoption if your business involves Burnaby property or development.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Stoney Creek development approvals may pause while watershed protection rules are reviewed
briefs.summary: City council has sent a request from the Stoney Creek Environment Committee to staff for review. The committee asked the city to create new rules to protect the Stoney Creek watershed (the land and waterways that drain into Stoney Creek) and to temporarily stop approving new development projects in that area while those rules are being developed.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to develop or build near Stoney Creek, check with the city before submitting any new development applications.

briefs.title: Stoney Creek construction impact rules under feasibility review — changes may follow
briefs.summary: City council has sent recommendations from the Environment Committee about reducing the impact of construction near Stoney Creek to staff for a feasibility review (a study to determine whether the ideas are practical and affordable). A report with findings will come back to council at a later date.
briefs.action_required: Monitor future council reports on Stoney Creek construction rules if you operate near the area.

briefs.title: City approves 2026 active transportation education and promotion initiatives
briefs.summary: City council has approved a set of education and promotion initiatives for 2026 focused on active transportation (walking, cycling, and other non-motorized travel). The initiatives were recommended by the Transportation Committee and will roll out through the coming year.

briefs.title: 49-Storey Mixed-Use Tower at Springer Avenue Moves Forward with Rezoning Approval
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used) for a 49-storey residential and retail tower at 2209 and 2225 Springer Avenue to move to first and second reading. The approval includes a housing agreement and a preliminary green light to sell City-owned land to make the project possible.
briefs.action_required: If your business operates near Springer Avenue, monitor future Council readings for construction and zoning impacts.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Burnaby Lake Village at 6800 Lougheed Highway rezoning moves to first reading
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used and built on) for 6800 Lougheed Highway to move forward for review. The update revises building heights, massing (how large and bulky buildings can be), and open space requirements as part of the Burnaby Lake Village Master Plan, bringing it in line with the city's long-term Official Community Plan 2050.
briefs.action_required: Monitor upcoming public hearings for this rezoning if your business operates near 6800 Lougheed Highway.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: 20-storey rental tower at 5058 Imperial Street replaces approved 6-storey plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has approved moving forward with a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used) for a 20-storey mixed-use market rental building at 5058 Imperial Street. This replaces a previously approved 6-storey plan for the same site, and the bylaw will now advance to its first and second formal readings.
briefs.action_required: If your business operates near 5058 Imperial Street, monitor rezoning progress for potential impacts.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: City-owned EV charging stations switching to energy-based fees under new bylaw amendments
briefs.summary: City council has approved a new pricing model for public electric vehicle (EV) charging stations it owns. Instead of paying by time, users will now be charged based on the amount of energy (electricity) they use. Three city bylaws are being updated to reflect this change.
briefs.action_required: If your business uses or manages city-owned EV chargers, review the new energy-based fee schedule once published.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: On-street public pay parking fees increased — higher costs for customers and deliveries
briefs.summary: City council has approved an increase to on-street public pay parking rates, as outlined in the Financial Management Committee report from February 17, 2026. Businesses that rely on street parking for customers, deliveries, or staff should expect higher parking costs in the area.
briefs.action_required: Review how higher street parking costs may affect customer visits and adjust pricing or delivery logistics if needed.

briefs.title: Heritage Alteration Permit granted for chimney work at Burnaby's W.J. Mathers House
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a Heritage Alteration Permit (official permission required before making changes to a protected historic building) for chimney alterations at the W.J. Mathers House 'Altnadene' at 6490 Deer Lake Avenue. This means the proposed chimney work at this heritage-designated property is now authorized to proceed.

briefs.title: City renews $670,000 Guardteck security contract for municipal sites and events
briefs.summary: City council approved a one-year contract renewal with Guardteck Security Corp to provide security officers at city-owned locations and special events, valued at approximately $670,000 including taxes. This means Guardteck remains the city's contracted security provider for the coming year.

briefs.title: City renews $162,400 portable toilet contract with Jiffy John Rentals
briefs.summary: Council approved a contract renewal with Jiffy John Rentals Ltd. to supply portable toilets at an estimated cost of $162,400 including taxes. This is a government procurement decision and does not create new rules or obligations for private business owners.

briefs.title: Burnaby Council approves up to 84 noise bylaw exemptions for outdoor events at three parks in 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved noise bylaw exemptions (official permission to exceed normal sound limits) for up to 84 outdoor events at Deer Lake Park, Central Park, and Swangard Stadium. These events can take place between March 15 and November 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to host or vend at events at these venues in 2026, confirm your event falls within the approved window.

key_dates.label: Burnaby outdoor event noise bylaw exemption window opens — Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard
Burnaby outdoor event noise bylaw exemption window closes — Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard

briefs.title: Burnaby Council approves Option B design for Landmark Burnaby Sign
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved the Option B design for the Landmark Burnaby Sign, directing staff to move forward with the project as described in the February 24, 2026 report. This is a civic branding initiative and does not create new rules or obligations for local businesses.

briefs.title: Burnaby freezes $63,005 FCM membership dues until city gets Big City Mayors' Caucus seat
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has paused payment of its 2026–2027 membership dues to the Federation of Canadian Municipalities (FCM), a national organization that lobbies the federal government on behalf of cities. The $63,005.33 payment is on hold until Burnaby is given a seat on the Big City Mayors' Caucus, a group within the FCM that gives larger cities a direct voice on national policy.

briefs.title: Commercial fleet parking permit renewed at 7000 Lougheed Highway
briefs.summary: Council approved the renewal of a Temporary Use Permit (a short-term permission that allows a property to be used in a way not normally allowed under its zoning) for commercial fleet vehicle parking and rentals at 7000 Lougheed Highway. This means the business at that address can continue operating fleet parking and rental activities on the site.

briefs.title: Antenna variance application at 9001 University Crescent sent back for further staff review
briefs.summary: A development variance permit (a formal request to bend standard building rules) for antenna equipment at 9001 University Crescent has been referred back to staff by Council. The application sought approval for antennas that exceed the allowed building face coverage and height limits, but a decision has been delayed pending further review.

briefs.title: Antenna variance application at 8888 University Drive sent back for further staff review
briefs.summary: A permit application (a formal request for permission to build outside standard rules) to install antenna equipment on a building at 8888 University Drive — covering variances for how much of the building face the equipment covers and how tall it can be — has been sent back to city staff for more review instead of being approved or denied.

briefs.title: Burnaby grants utility access rights over 3860 Sperling Avenue for Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a statutory right-of-way (a legal permission allowing utility companies to access and use a strip of private or city-owned land) over part of 3860 Sperling Avenue. This allows third-party utility providers to install and maintain services needed for the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre development.

briefs.title: Construction permits paused in Lower Westridge as city launches Natural Hazard safety review
briefs.summary: City council has started a city-wide Natural Hazard Development Permit Area (DPA) program — a set of rules that controls where and how buildings can be built in areas at risk from natural hazards like landslides or flooding. Council approved $120,000 to assess risks in the Lower Westridge neighbourhood and asked the Province to pause new construction permits there until the results are in.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to build or develop in Lower Westridge, check with the city before submitting any permit applications.

briefs.title: Burnaby now requires a special business licence condition to sell bear spray at retail
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has amended its Business Licence Bylaw and Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw to regulate the retail sale of bear spray. Stores that sell bear spray in Burnaby must now meet new rules tied to their business licence (the permit that allows them to legally operate).
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby to confirm what licence conditions apply to your bear spray sales.

briefs.title: Burnaby North Road BIA renewed for 2026–2031 with new bylaw coming
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a five-year renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Association (BIA — a group that collects fees from local businesses to fund area improvements) for 2026–2031. A new BIA bylaw is being drafted and will be brought forward for formal approval.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is in the North Road BIA zone — you may owe BIA fees starting 2026.

briefs.title: Burnaby plumbing permit fees updated — check new costs before your next project
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved small changes to plumbing permit fees under the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (the city's official list of fees for permits and services). The City Solicitor has been directed to prepare the formal bylaw amendment to make these new fees official.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated Burnaby plumbing permit fee schedule before applying for your next plumbing permit.

briefs.title: City will now remove outdated land-use restrictions from private properties on request
briefs.summary: City Council approved the removal of old restrictive covenants (legally binding rules attached to a property's title that limit how it can be used) that the City itself had placed on private properties. Property owners can now ask the City Solicitor to formally remove these outdated restrictions from their title.
briefs.action_required: If your property has a City-imposed restrictive covenant on its title, contact the City Solicitor's office to request its removal.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
